functions.name: ST_Contains
functions.summary: Returns true when B lies entirely inside A and at least one point of B is in the interior of A. That second clause is the part that surprises people: a point sitting exactly on A's boundary is not contained, and neither is a line that runs entirely along A's edge. Use it for point-in-polygon assignment and for "which region does this feature fall in" when you are happy for boundary cases to be excluded; use ST_Covers when you are not. Since PostGIS 2.3 the point-in-polygon case has a short-circuit path that makes it noticeably faster than the general DE-9IM evaluation.

arguments.name: A
arguments.description: The candidate container. In a table query this is normally the polygon column.

arguments.name: B
arguments.description: The candidate contained geometry.

example.sql:
SELECT ST_Contains('POLYGON((0 0, 0 10, 10 10, 10 0, 0 0))'::geometry,
                   'POINT(5 5)'::geometry) AS interior_point,
       ST_Contains('POLYGON((0 0, 0 10, 10 10, 10 0, 0 0))'::geometry,
                   'POINT(0 5)'::geometry) AS boundary_point;
example.psycopg:
with conn.cursor() as cur:
    cur.execute(
        """
        SELECT d.district_code
        FROM districts d
        WHERE ST_Contains(d.geom, ST_SetSRID(ST_MakePoint(%s, %s), 4326))
        """,
        (lon, lat),
    )
    district = cur.fetchone()
example.geoalchemy:
from sqlalchemy import select
from geoalchemy2.functions import ST_Contains, ST_MakePoint, ST_SetSRID

here = ST_SetSRID(ST_MakePoint(lon, lat), 4326)
stmt = select(District.district_code).where(ST_Contains(District.geom, here))

functions.srid_notes: Mixed SRIDs error rather than reproject. The test is planar, so a polygon defined in 4326 is treated as a straight-edged shape in degree space; for very large administrative polygons the straight-line edges differ from the geodesic ones and a point near an edge can flip. There is no geography form of ST_Contains at all - cast to geometry, or use ST_Covers, which does have one.

index_usage.notes: Emits an && term on A's box containing B's box, so a GiST index on the polygon column is used directly. For a handful of very complex polygons, ST_Subdivide-ing them first shrinks the boxes and dramatically improves the index's selectivity.

common_mistakes: Expecting a point exactly on the polygon edge to be contained. It is not - ST_Contains requires interior intersection, ST_Covers does not.
Getting the argument order backwards. ST_Contains(container, thing) is the reverse of ST_Within(thing, container).
Reaching for a geography version. None exists; ST_Covers is the geography-capable near-equivalent.

functions.name: ST_ContainsProperly
functions.summary: Returns true when B intersects the interior of A but touches neither A's boundary nor its exterior - strict interior containment. The practical difference from ST_Contains is that anything grazing the edge is rejected, including A itself: a geometry does not properly contain itself, because its own boundary is not interior. It is also measurably faster than ST_Contains when you test one prepared polygon against many candidates, which is why it appears in tuned batch clipping and tiling queries.

arguments.name: A
arguments.description: The candidate container; only its interior counts.

arguments.name: B
arguments.description: The candidate contained geometry, which must avoid A's boundary entirely.

example.sql:
SELECT ST_ContainsProperly('POLYGON((0 0, 0 10, 10 10, 10 0, 0 0))'::geometry,
                           'POINT(5 5)'::geometry) AS interior_point,
       ST_ContainsProperly('POLYGON((0 0, 0 10, 10 10, 10 0, 0 0))'::geometry,
                           'POLYGON((0 0, 0 10, 10 10, 10 0, 0 0))'::geometry) AS itself;
example.psycopg:
with conn.cursor() as cur:
    cur.execute(
        """
        SELECT b.id
        FROM buildings b
        WHERE ST_ContainsProperly(%s::geometry, b.geom)
        """,
        (tile_ewkt,),
    )
    fully_inside = [row[0] for row in cur]
example.geoalchemy:
from sqlalchemy import select
from geoalchemy2.functions import ST_ContainsProperly

stmt = select(Building.id).where(ST_ContainsProperly(tile_geom, Building.geom))

functions.srid_notes: Same mixed-SRID error and same planar semantics as ST_Contains, and likewise there is no geography form. Because the predicate hinges on exact boundary coincidence, it is unusually sensitive to coordinate precision: reprojecting one side with ST_Transform can move a shared vertex by a few floating-point units and turn a false into a true.

index_usage.notes: Index-assisted through the same && rewrite as ST_Contains. Its real advantage shows in tiling workloads where one constant polygon is tested against many rows and GEOS can reuse a prepared version of it.

common_mistakes: Assuming ST_ContainsProperly(A, A) is true. It is false, because A's boundary is not part of A's interior.
Using it for ordinary point-in-polygon lookups, where it silently drops points that landed exactly on a shared district edge.
Treating it as a drop-in speed-up for ST_Contains. The results differ for every boundary-touching case.

functions.name: ST_CoveredBy
functions.summary: Returns true when no point of A lies outside B - ST_Covers with the arguments reversed. It stands to ST_Within exactly as ST_Covers stands to ST_Contains: same shape of test, but boundary contact counts. Use it for "is this feature entirely inside my area of interest" when features legitimately abut the boundary, and to keep the indexed column in the leading position.

arguments.name: A
arguments.description: The covered geometry. Put your indexed column here.

arguments.name: B
arguments.description: The covering geometry, typically a constant area of interest.

example.sql:
SELECT ST_CoveredBy('POINT(0 5)'::geometry,
                    'POLYGON((0 0, 0 10, 10 10, 10 0, 0 0))'::geometry) AS covered,
       ST_Within('POINT(0 5)'::geometry,
                 'POLYGON((0 0, 0 10, 10 10, 10 0, 0 0))'::geometry) AS within;
example.psycopg:
with conn.cursor() as cur:
    cur.execute(
        """
        SELECT count(*)
        FROM cadastral_lots
        WHERE ST_CoveredBy(geom, %s::geometry)
        """,
        (survey_area_ewkt,),
    )
    (fully_inside,) = cur.fetchone()
example.geoalchemy:
from sqlalchemy import func, select
from geoalchemy2.functions import ST_CoveredBy

stmt = (
    select(func.count())
    .select_from(CadastralLot)
    .where(ST_CoveredBy(CadastralLot.geom, survey_area))
)

functions.srid_notes: Mixed SRIDs error in the geometry form. This predicate is the one most often used against a hand-drawn area of interest arriving from a web client in 4326 while the table sits in a projected SRID - transform the incoming polygon once, not the column, or the GiST index goes unused. The geography form measures containment on the spheroid with geodesic edges.

index_usage.notes: Index-assisted on A through an internal box-containment term. Both geometry and geography columns are supported, and the leading-argument position is what lets the planner pick the index on your table.

common_mistakes: Confusing it with ST_Covers and getting exactly inverted results with no error.
Transforming the indexed column instead of the constant polygon, which disables the index.
Assuming it is the same as ST_Within. It differs precisely for features that touch the boundary.

functions.name: ST_Covers
functions.summary: Returns true when no point of B lies outside A. This is the containment test most people actually mean: unlike ST_Contains it has no interior-intersection requirement, so a point on A's boundary is covered, and a line running along A's edge is covered. It is also slightly cheaper to evaluate than ST_Contains because it needs a simpler DE-9IM check, and it is the only containment predicate with a geography implementation. Prefer it whenever boundary cases in your data are real - shared administrative edges, snapped-to-edge points, parcels that abut their district outline.

arguments.name: A
arguments.description: The covering geometry.

arguments.name: B
arguments.description: The covered geometry; no part of it may fall outside A.

example.sql:
SELECT ST_Covers('POLYGON((0 0, 0 10, 10 10, 10 0, 0 0))'::geometry,
                 'POINT(0 5)'::geometry) AS covers_edge,
       ST_Contains('POLYGON((0 0, 0 10, 10 10, 10 0, 0 0))'::geometry,
                   'POINT(0 5)'::geometry) AS contains_edge;
example.psycopg:
with conn.cursor() as cur:
    cur.execute(
        """
        SELECT c.name
        FROM countries c
        WHERE ST_Covers(c.geog, ST_MakePoint(%s, %s)::geography)
        """,
        (lon, lat),
    )
    country = cur.fetchone()
example.geoalchemy:
from sqlalchemy import select
from geoalchemy2 import Geography
from geoalchemy2.functions import ST_Covers, ST_MakePoint

here = ST_MakePoint(lon, lat).cast(Geography)
stmt = select(Country.name).where(ST_Covers(Country.geog, here))

functions.srid_notes: The geometry form is planar and errors on mixed SRIDs. The geography form assumes lon/lat on the spheroid (SRID 4326) and follows geodesic edges, so a coastline polygon can cover a point under geography that the planar test misses, or vice versa, wherever edges span many degrees. Do not mix the two forms in one query and expect identical answers near long edges.

index_usage.notes: Index-assisted via the usual && rewrite, for both geometry and geography. Geography GiST indexes are built on geodetic boxes, so the same query shape works unchanged against a geography column.

common_mistakes: Reaching for ST_Contains out of habit and then losing rows whose points were snapped exactly onto a shared boundary.
Assuming the geometry and geography forms agree. They use different edge models and disagree near long or high-latitude edges.
Reversing the arguments; ST_Covers(container, thing) is the mirror of ST_CoveredBy(thing, container).

functions.name: ST_Crosses
functions.summary: Returns true when the interiors of two geometries intersect, but the intersection has a lower dimension than the higher-dimensional of the two inputs, and neither contains the other. In practice that means a line crossing another line at a point, or a line passing through a polygon's interior and out the other side. It is the predicate for "this route crosses that boundary" checks - pipelines crossing property lines, rivers crossing administrative borders. Two polygons can never cross, and two points can never cross.

arguments.name: A
arguments.description: First geometry, typically a line.

arguments.name: B
arguments.description: Second geometry, typically another line or a polygon.

example.sql:
SELECT ST_Crosses('LINESTRING(0 0, 10 10)'::geometry,
                  'LINESTRING(0 10, 10 0)'::geometry) AS lines_cross,
       ST_Crosses('POLYGON((0 0, 0 10, 10 10, 10 0, 0 0))'::geometry,
                  'POLYGON((5 5, 5 15, 15 15, 15 5, 5 5))'::geometry) AS polys_cross;
example.psycopg:
with conn.cursor() as cur:
    cur.execute(
        """
        SELECT p.id, b.name
        FROM pipelines p
        JOIN boundaries b ON ST_Crosses(p.geom, b.geom)
        WHERE p.id = %s
        """,
        (pipeline_id,),
    )
    crossings = cur.fetchall()
example.geoalchemy:
from sqlalchemy import select
from geoalchemy2.functions import ST_Crosses

stmt = (
    select(Pipeline.id, Boundary.name)
    .join(Boundary, ST_Crosses(Pipeline.geom, Boundary.geom))
    .where(Pipeline.id == pipeline_id)
)

functions.srid_notes: Planar, SRID units, mixed SRIDs error. Crossing is a topological fact rather than a metric one, so it is insensitive to the choice of projection in principle - but the straight segments of a 4326 linestring follow a different path on the ground than the geodesics they represent, so a long segment can cross a boundary in degree space that it misses in reality. ST_Segmentize the line before testing if the segments span more than a degree or so.

index_usage.notes: Index-assisted via the usual && rewrite, but boxes are a weak filter for long diagonal lines: a single motorway's box may overlap most of the country. Subdividing long linework into shorter segments improves selectivity far more than any index tuning.

common_mistakes: Using it for polygon-polygon overlap. Two polygons never cross; that case is ST_Overlaps.
Expecting a line that merely ends on a polygon boundary to cross it - it touches, it does not cross.
Testing very long unsegmentised 4326 linestrings, where the planar chord and the true geodesic disagree about which boundaries are crossed.

functions.name: ST_DWithin
signatures: ST_DWithin(geometry A, geometry B, double precision distance) -> boolean
ST_DWithin(geography A, geography B, double precision distance_meters, boolean use_spheroid = true) -> boolean
functions.summary: Returns true when the two geometries are within the given distance of each other, measured between their closest points. It is the correct way to write a radius filter: unlike a bare "ST_Distance(a, b) < r" it expands the bounding box by the search distance and so stays index-assisted. Use it for proximity joins, "everything within 500 m of this address", and as the WHERE-clause partner to the <-> KNN operator in ORDER BY. A distance of 0 makes it equivalent to ST_Intersects.

arguments.name: A
arguments.description: First geometry or geography; put the indexed column here.

arguments.name: B
arguments.description: Second geometry or geography, usually the reference feature.

arguments.name: distance
arguments.description: Search radius, in SRID units for geometry and in metres for geography.

arguments.name: use_spheroid
arguments.description: Geography form only. False uses the faster sphere instead of the spheroid.

example.sql:
SELECT ST_DWithin('POINT(0 0)'::geometry, 'POINT(3 4)'::geometry, 5) AS within_5,
       ST_DWithin('POINT(0 0)'::geometry, 'POINT(3 4)'::geometry, 4.9) AS within_4_9;
example.psycopg:
with conn.cursor() as cur:
    cur.execute(
        """
        SELECT id, name
        FROM stops
        WHERE ST_DWithin(geog, ST_MakePoint(%s, %s)::geography, %s)
        ORDER BY geog <-> ST_MakePoint(%s, %s)::geography
        LIMIT 20
        """,
        (lon, lat, radius_m, lon, lat),
    )
    nearby = cur.fetchall()
example.geoalchemy:
from sqlalchemy import select
from geoalchemy2 import Geography
from geoalchemy2.functions import ST_DWithin, ST_MakePoint

here = ST_MakePoint(lon, lat).cast(Geography)
stmt = select(Stop.id, Stop.name).where(ST_DWithin(Stop.geog, here, radius_m)).limit(20)

functions.srid_notes: This is where the units bite hardest. On geometry the distance is in the SRID's units, so ST_DWithin(geom, pt, 500) on SRID 4326 means 500 degrees, not 500 metres - either store a projected column or cast both sides to geography. On geography the distance is always metres regardless of the underlying SRID, and use_spheroid=false trades a little accuracy for speed. Cast both sides explicitly rather than one: the two overloads mean different things by the same number, so a half-cast call is ambiguous to the reader and easy to get wrong. Note too that wrapping an indexed geometry column in ::geography stops a geometry GiST index applying - you need an index on the geography column or expression.

index_usage.notes: Fully index-assisted: PostGIS rewrites it as an && against the reference box expanded by the distance, then rechecks exactly. Do not hand-write ST_Expand plus && around it - the planner already does that. Keep the indexed column as the first argument.

common_mistakes: Writing ST_Distance(a, b) < r instead, which cannot use the index and scans the whole table.
Passing metres to the geometry form on SRID 4326, where the argument is degrees - 500 there is effectively the whole planet.
Casting the indexed column to geography inside the call. That is a function on the column and kills the geometry index; add a geography column or index the cast expression.

functions.name: ST_Equals
functions.summary: Returns true when two geometries occupy the same set of points, regardless of how those points are ordered or how many vertices are used to describe them. A linestring and its reverse are equal; a polygon whose ring starts at a different vertex is equal. This is spatial equality, not binary equality, so use it when comparing geometry that has passed through different pipelines, and use the = operator or a hash of ST_AsBinary when you need to detect byte-level changes instead.

arguments.name: A
arguments.description: First geometry.

arguments.name: B
arguments.description: Second geometry.

example.psycopg:
with conn.cursor() as cur:
    cur.execute(
        """
        SELECT id
        FROM boundaries_current c
        WHERE EXISTS (
            SELECT 1 FROM boundaries_previous p
            WHERE p.id = c.id AND NOT ST_Equals(p.geom, c.geom)
        )
        """
    )
    changed_ids = [row[0] for row in cur]
example.geoalchemy:
from sqlalchemy import select
from geoalchemy2.functions import ST_Equals

stmt = (
    select(Current.id)
    .join(Previous, Previous.id == Current.id)
    .where(~ST_Equals(Previous.geom, Current.geom))
)

functions.srid_notes: Mixed SRIDs error, so two geometries describing the same place in different projections are never comparable without an explicit ST_Transform - and after transforming, floating-point drift usually means they are no longer exactly equal either. For cross-projection comparison use ST_SnapToGrid on both sides first, or compare ST_Distance against a tolerance rather than relying on exact equality.

index_usage.notes: Index-assisted, despite the documentation page being silent about it. ST_Equals carries the same postgis_index_supportfn as ST_Intersects, and the term it emits is ~= (identical bounding box) rather than the && used by the other predicates - a tighter filter, since two equal geometries must have the same box. Verified on PostGIS 3.4.3 with EXPLAIN ANALYZE over a 5000-row GiST-indexed table: "Bitmap Index Scan on cells_gix, Index Cond: (geom ~= '...'::geometry)". An explicit && prefilter is therefore redundant. Note that the = operator on geometry is a different thing entirely - it compares serialised representations and is what btree indexes and DISTINCT use. Contrast ST_Disjoint and ST_Relate, which genuinely have no support function and always scan.

common_mistakes: Confusing ST_Equals with the = operator. The former is spatial, the latter is byte-level, and they disagree on reversed or renumbered rings.
Expecting it to survive a round trip through ST_Transform, where floating-point drift breaks exact equality.
Using it on geometries with different vertex counts along the same line and being surprised it returns true - collinear extra vertices do not change the point set.

functions.name: ST_Intersects
functions.summary: Returns true when two geometries share any point at all, whether that point is in an interior or on a boundary. It is the workhorse predicate of spatial SQL and the right default for "does this feature touch that feature" and for joining two layers together, because it is the union of contains, within, touches, crosses and overlaps. Reach for it unless you specifically need to exclude boundary-only contact, in which case use ST_Overlaps or ST_ContainsProperly. It is the exact logical negation of ST_Disjoint but, unlike ST_Disjoint, it is index-assisted.

arguments.name: A
arguments.description: First geometry; when written against a table this should be the indexed column.

arguments.name: B
arguments.description: Second geometry, commonly a constant, parameter or the other side of a join.

example.sql:
SELECT ST_Intersects('LINESTRING(0 0, 10 10)'::geometry, 'POINT(5 5)'::geometry) AS on_line,
       ST_Intersects('LINESTRING(0 0, 10 10)'::geometry, 'POINT(9 1)'::geometry) AS off_line;
example.psycopg:
with conn.cursor() as cur:
    cur.execute(
        """
        SELECT p.id, z.name
        FROM parcels p
        JOIN flood_zones z ON ST_Intersects(p.geom, z.geom)
        WHERE p.id = %s
        """,
        (parcel_id,),
    )
    zones = cur.fetchall()
example.geoalchemy:
from sqlalchemy import select
from geoalchemy2.functions import ST_Intersects

stmt = (
    select(Parcel.id, FloodZone.name)
    .join(FloodZone, ST_Intersects(Parcel.geom, FloodZone.geom))
    .where(Parcel.id == parcel_id)
)

functions.srid_notes: Both arguments must share an SRID or the call errors with "Operation on mixed SRID geometries"; there is no implicit reprojection. The geometry form is purely planar, so on 4326 two features either share a coordinate or they do not - latitude distortion is irrelevant to a yes/no intersection test. The geography form does use great-circle edges, which means a long segment between two lon/lat points can intersect a polygon under geography that it misses under geometry.

index_usage.notes: ST_Intersects automatically emits an && term against the bounding boxes, so a GiST index on either column is used without any extra help. Adding your own && alongside it is redundant. Wrapping the indexed column in ST_Transform or ST_Buffer defeats the index - transform the constant side instead.

common_mistakes: Adding a hand-written && next to ST_Intersects. The planner already generates that term; the duplicate only costs planning time.
Calling ST_Transform on the indexed column to match the other SRID, which forces a sequential scan. Transform the literal, or store a second projected column.
Assuming it excludes boundary-only contact. Two polygons that merely share an edge do intersect; use ST_Overlaps if you want genuine area overlap.

functions.name: ST_Overlaps
functions.summary: Returns true when two geometries of the same dimension share some but not all of their interior space - they intersect, but neither contains the other and the intersection has the same dimension as the inputs. This is the predicate for genuine partial overlap, which is what people usually mean when they say two polygons overlap and mistakenly reach for ST_Intersects. It is the natural test for detecting conflicting land use, double-counted coverage areas, or geometry that should have been dissolved.

arguments.name: A
arguments.description: First geometry.

arguments.name: B
arguments.description: Second geometry, which must have the same dimension as A for overlap to be possible.

example.sql:
SELECT ST_Overlaps('POLYGON((0 0, 0 10, 10 10, 10 0, 0 0))'::geometry,
                   'POLYGON((5 5, 5 15, 15 15, 15 5, 5 5))'::geometry) AS partial,
       ST_Overlaps('POLYGON((0 0, 0 10, 10 10, 10 0, 0 0))'::geometry,
                   'POLYGON((2 2, 2 4, 4 4, 4 2, 2 2))'::geometry) AS nested;
example.psycopg:
with conn.cursor() as cur:
    cur.execute(
        """
        SELECT a.id, b.id
        FROM service_areas a
        JOIN service_areas b ON a.id < b.id AND ST_Overlaps(a.geom, b.geom)
        """
    )
    conflicts = cur.fetchall()
example.geoalchemy:
from sqlalchemy import select
from sqlalchemy.orm import aliased
from geoalchemy2.functions import ST_Overlaps

b = aliased(ServiceArea)
stmt = (
    select(ServiceArea.id, b.id)
    .join(b, (ServiceArea.id < b.id) & ST_Overlaps(ServiceArea.geom, b.geom))
)

functions.srid_notes: Planar and SRID-strict, with no geography form. Overlap detection is often run on data merged from several sources; if those sources arrived in different SRIDs and were transformed into a common one, tiny reprojection slivers appear along shared edges and ST_Overlaps will report them as real overlaps. Snap or clean with ST_SnapToGrid and check ST_Area of the intersection before trusting the result.

index_usage.notes: Index-assisted via the standard && rewrite. All-pairs overlap detection on a large table is inherently quadratic in the candidate set, so add an a.id < b.id guard to halve the work and consider ST_Subdivide on the largest polygons.

common_mistakes: Using ST_Intersects when you meant ST_Overlaps, which then also reports nested and edge-touching pairs.
Testing geometries of different dimensions - a line and a polygon can never overlap, only cross or intersect.
Treating sliver overlaps from reprojection as real; filter by ST_Area of ST_Intersection above a tolerance.

functions.name: ST_Touches
functions.summary: Returns true when two geometries share at least one boundary point but their interiors do not intersect at all. It is the predicate for adjacency: neighbouring parcels, districts sharing a border, road segments meeting end to end. Because it requires disjoint interiors, two overlapping polygons never touch, and because it requires a shared point, two separated polygons never touch either. Two points can never touch each other, since points have no boundary.

arguments.name: A
arguments.description: First geometry; its interior must not meet B's.

arguments.name: B
arguments.description: Second geometry, sharing only boundary points with A.

example.psycopg:
with conn.cursor() as cur:
    cur.execute(
        """
        SELECT b.id
        FROM parcels a
        JOIN parcels b ON ST_Touches(a.geom, b.geom) AND a.id <> b.id
        WHERE a.id = %s
        """,
        (parcel_id,),
    )
    neighbours = [row[0] for row in cur]
example.geoalchemy:
from sqlalchemy import select
from sqlalchemy.orm import aliased
from geoalchemy2.functions import ST_Touches

other = aliased(Parcel)
stmt = (
    select(other.id)
    .join(other, ST_Touches(Parcel.geom, other.geom))
    .where(Parcel.id == parcel_id, other.id != parcel_id)
)

functions.srid_notes: Planar and SRID-strict. Adjacency is entirely a question of exact coordinate coincidence, so this predicate is the most fragile one under reprojection: ST_Transform introduces floating-point drift that can pull a shared vertex apart by a fraction of a unit and turn neighbours into non-neighbours. Build adjacency in the SRID the data was digitised in, and consider ST_SnapToGrid on both sides if the source came from different vendors.

index_usage.notes: Index-assisted through the standard && rewrite. Self-joins on a whole parcel table are still expensive because every neighbouring box overlaps; restrict by area or process in batches rather than materialising the full adjacency graph in one statement.

common_mistakes: Expecting overlapping polygons to touch. Any interior intersection makes ST_Touches false.
Relying on it across differently projected copies of the same data, where rounding has already broken the shared vertices.
Forgetting the a.id <> b.id guard in a self-join, though a geometry never touches itself so this mostly costs work rather than correctness.

functions.name: ST_Within
functions.summary: Returns true when A lies completely inside B, with at least one point of A in B's interior. It is exactly ST_Contains with the arguments swapped, and it exists so you can write the query in whichever direction reads naturally - and, more usefully, so the indexed column can sit in the first position. As with ST_Contains, a geometry that only touches B's boundary is not within it.

arguments.name: A
arguments.description: The candidate inner geometry. Put your indexed column here.

arguments.name: B
arguments.description: The candidate container, often a constant envelope or an area of interest.

example.sql:
SELECT ST_Within('POINT(5 5)'::geometry,
                 'POLYGON((0 0, 0 10, 10 10, 10 0, 0 0))'::geometry) AS inside,
       ST_Within('POINT(0 5)'::geometry,
                 'POLYGON((0 0, 0 10, 10 10, 10 0, 0 0))'::geometry) AS on_edge;
example.psycopg:
with conn.cursor() as cur:
    cur.execute(
        """
        SELECT id, species
        FROM trees
        WHERE ST_Within(geom, ST_MakeEnvelope(%s, %s, %s, %s, 27700))
        """,
        (xmin, ymin, xmax, ymax),
    )
    in_view = cur.fetchall()
example.geoalchemy:
from sqlalchemy import select
from geoalchemy2.functions import ST_MakeEnvelope, ST_Within

box = ST_MakeEnvelope(xmin, ymin, xmax, ymax, 27700)
stmt = select(Tree.id, Tree.species).where(ST_Within(Tree.geom, box))

functions.srid_notes: Planar, SRID units, mixed SRIDs raise an error. When filtering to a viewport, build the envelope in the column's own SRID with ST_MakeEnvelope(..., srid) rather than transforming the column - the latter is correct but unindexable. No geography form exists; ST_CoveredBy is the geography-capable alternative.

index_usage.notes: Generates an @ box-containment term internally, so a GiST index on A is used when B is a constant or parameter. This is the idiomatic spelling for viewport and area-of-interest filters against an indexed geometry column.

common_mistakes: Swapping the arguments with ST_Contains, which returns wrong rows silently rather than erroring.
Using ST_Within for a viewport clip when you actually want partially visible features too - that is ST_Intersects.
Expecting features flush against the area-of-interest boundary to be included; they are not, use ST_CoveredBy.

functions.summary: Returns true when the 2D bounding boxes of the two arguments intersect. This is the raw index operator: it compares cached boxes, never the actual vertices, so it is cheap but approximate. Every GiST-accelerated predicate in PostGIS is internally a && test followed by an exact recheck, which is why && on its own is the fastest possible spatial filter and also the least precise one.

arguments.name: A
arguments.description: Left operand; its cached 2D bounding box is used.

arguments.name: B
arguments.description: Right operand; its cached 2D bounding box is used.

example.sql:
SELECT 'LINESTRING(0 0, 10 10)'::geometry && 'POINT(9 1)'::geometry AS bbox_hit,
       ST_Intersects('LINESTRING(0 0, 10 10)'::geometry, 'POINT(9 1)'::geometry) AS real_hit;
example.psycopg:
with conn.cursor() as cur:
    cur.execute(
        "SELECT id FROM parcels WHERE geom && ST_MakeEnvelope(%s, %s, %s, %s, 3857)",
        (xmin, ymin, xmax, ymax),
    )
    candidate_ids = [row[0] for row in cur]
example.geoalchemy:
from sqlalchemy import select
from geoalchemy2.functions import ST_MakeEnvelope

box = ST_MakeEnvelope(xmin, ymin, xmax, ymax, 3857)
stmt = select(Parcel.id).where(Parcel.geom.bool_op("&&")(box))

functions.srid_notes: Both operands must share an SRID or PostGIS raises "Operation on mixed SRID geometries". The comparison is always planar and always in the units of that SRID, so on 4326 a box is degrees, not metres. Boxes of geographic data that cross the antimeridian wrap badly - split such geometries before indexing.

index_usage.notes: This is the index operator itself. A GiST index on the left column is used directly whenever the right operand is a constant or a parameter.

common_mistakes: Treating && as a real intersection test. It only compares rectangles, so a diagonal line 'intersects' every point in its bounding box.
Mixing SRIDs between the operands, which errors out rather than silently reprojecting.
Adding && alongside ST_Intersects in the same WHERE clause. ST_Intersects already emits the && term itself, so the extra predicate only costs planning time.

functions.summary: The n-dimensional counterpart of &&: it returns true when the n-D bounding boxes of the two geometries intersect, taking Z (and M, for the ND index) into account. You need it when a 2D box test would wrongly match features that are separated only in elevation, such as building floors or seismic horizons.

arguments.name: A
arguments.description: Left operand; its n-D bounding box is used.

arguments.name: B
arguments.description: Right operand; its n-D bounding box is used.

example.sql:
SELECT 'LINESTRING Z (0 0 0, 1 1 1)'::geometry &&& 'LINESTRING Z (0 0 5, 1 1 6)'::geometry AS nd_hit,
       'LINESTRING Z (0 0 0, 1 1 1)'::geometry &&  'LINESTRING Z (0 0 5, 1 1 6)'::geometry AS twod_hit;
example.psycopg:
with conn.cursor() as cur:
    cur.execute(
        "SELECT id FROM boreholes WHERE geom &&& ST_3DMakeBox(%s::geometry, %s::geometry)",
        (lower_corner_ewkt, upper_corner_ewkt),
    )
    hits = [row[0] for row in cur]

functions.srid_notes: Same mixed-SRID rule as &&. Z values are in whatever unit the source data used and are never converted by ST_Transform, which only touches X/Y for most projections - so a table in metres horizontally may still hold feet vertically. Check before trusting a Z comparison.

index_usage.notes: Requires an n-D index built with the ND operator class: CREATE INDEX ... USING gist (geom gist_geometry_ops_nd). The default 2D GiST index will not serve &&&.

common_mistakes: Expecting the default GiST index to serve it; you must create a gist_geometry_ops_nd index.
Using &&& on 2D data, where it degenerates to && with extra confusion.
Assuming ST_Transform reprojects Z, so comparing Z values across differently projected tables.

functions.summary: Returns the distance between the bounding boxes of two geometries, and like <-> it can drive an index-assisted ORDER BY. Because it compares boxes it is cheaper than <-> but only approximate; it returns 0 for any pair whose boxes overlap, so it cannot rank objects that are box-wise nested.

arguments.name: A
arguments.description: Left operand; only its bounding box is considered.

arguments.name: B
arguments.description: Right operand; only its bounding box is considered.

example.sql:
SELECT 'LINESTRING(0 0, 1 1)'::geometry <#> 'POINT(4 0)'::geometry AS box_dist,
       ST_Distance('LINESTRING(0 0, 1 1)'::geometry, 'POINT(4 0)'::geometry) AS true_dist;
example.psycopg:
with conn.cursor() as cur:
    cur.execute(
        """
        SELECT id FROM tiles
        ORDER BY bbox <#> ST_SetSRID(ST_MakePoint(%s, %s), 3857)
        LIMIT 50
        """,
        (x, y),
    )
    coarse_candidates = [row[0] for row in cur]
example.geoalchemy:
from sqlalchemy import select
from geoalchemy2.functions import ST_MakePoint, ST_SetSRID

here = ST_SetSRID(ST_MakePoint(x, y), 3857)
stmt = select(Tile.id).order_by(Tile.bbox.distance_box(here)).limit(50)

functions.srid_notes: Planar and in SRID units, exactly like &&. There is no geography form of <#>. If your data is in 4326 the box distance is in degrees and is anisotropic away from the equator, which makes it a poor ranking key over large latitude ranges.

index_usage.notes: Index-assisted in ORDER BY with LIMIT, same conditions as <->. Useful as a cheap first pass over large polygons, then re-rank the shortlist with ST_Distance.

common_mistakes: Expecting true distance. Two long overlapping-box geometries both score 0 and become unorderable.
Using it on point data, where it is identical to <-> but less obvious to the next reader.
Assuming a geography variant exists; it does not.

functions.summary: The KNN distance operator. In an ORDER BY it lets a GiST index return rows in order of increasing distance from a reference point, which is the only way to get a nearest-neighbour query that does not read the whole table. Since PostGIS 2.2 on PostgreSQL 9.5+ it returns true geometry-to-geometry distance, not box distance.

arguments.name: A
arguments.description: Left operand. For index use, this must be the indexed column.

arguments.name: B
arguments.description: Right operand, normally a constant reference geometry.

example.psycopg:
with conn.cursor() as cur:
    cur.execute(
        """
        SELECT id, name, geom <-> ST_SetSRID(ST_MakePoint(%s, %s), 4326) AS dist
        FROM stops
        ORDER BY geom <-> ST_SetSRID(ST_MakePoint(%s, %s), 4326)
        LIMIT 10
        """,
        (lon, lat, lon, lat),
    )
    nearest = cur.fetchall()
example.geoalchemy:
from sqlalchemy import select
from geoalchemy2.functions import ST_MakePoint, ST_SetSRID

here = ST_SetSRID(ST_MakePoint(lon, lat), 4326)
stmt = select(Stop.id, Stop.name).order_by(Stop.geom.distance_centroid(here)).limit(10)

functions.srid_notes: On geometry the result is in SRID units, so on 4326 you get degrees - useless as a distance but perfectly valid as an ordering key at small extents. For metres either cast both sides to geography (the geography <-> is also index-assisted) or store a projected column. Ordering by degrees and ordering by metres differ noticeably at high latitudes.

index_usage.notes: Index-assisted only in an ORDER BY with a LIMIT, where one side is a constant and the other is the indexed column. Put it in a WHERE clause and you get a sequential scan; use ST_DWithin there instead.

common_mistakes: Using <-> in WHERE (geom <-> point < 1000). That is not index-assisted - use ST_DWithin for a radius filter.
Omitting LIMIT, which makes the planner prefer a sort over the index scan and reads every row.
Reading the geometry result as metres on SRID 4326, where it is degrees.

functions.summary: Returns true when the bounding box of A is completely contained by the bounding box of B. It is exactly the ~ operator with the operands reversed, provided so that the indexed column can stay on the left in either direction of a containment query.

arguments.name: A
arguments.description: Candidate contained geometry; its box must fall inside B's.

arguments.name: B
arguments.description: Candidate container.

example.psycopg:
with conn.cursor() as cur:
    cur.execute(
        "SELECT count(*) FROM trees WHERE geom @ ST_MakeEnvelope(%s, %s, %s, %s, 27700)",
        (xmin, ymin, xmax, ymax),
    )
    (count,) = cur.fetchone()
example.geoalchemy:
from sqlalchemy import func, select
from geoalchemy2.functions import ST_MakeEnvelope

box = ST_MakeEnvelope(xmin, ymin, xmax, ymax, 27700)
stmt = select(func.count()).select_from(Tree).where(Tree.geom.bool_op("@")(box))

functions.srid_notes: Planar, SRID units, mixed SRIDs error. For a viewport query the envelope you build must be in the same SRID as the column - build it with ST_MakeEnvelope(..., srid) rather than transforming the column, which would defeat the index.

index_usage.notes: Index-assisted on the left operand, which makes it the natural spelling for "features inside this viewport" against an indexed geometry column.

common_mistakes: Swapping the operands, which silently returns the wrong rows rather than erroring.
Wrapping the indexed column in ST_Transform to match the envelope's SRID, which makes the index unusable.
Expecting exact containment; only the boxes are compared.

functions.summary: Returns true when the bounding box of A completely contains the bounding box of B. It is the box-level analogue of ST_Contains and is occasionally useful as a hand-written prefilter for containment queries against very complex polygons, where the exact test is expensive.

arguments.name: A
arguments.description: Candidate container; its bounding box must enclose B's.

arguments.name: B
arguments.description: Candidate contained geometry.

example.psycopg:
with conn.cursor() as cur:
    cur.execute(
        "SELECT id FROM regions WHERE geom ~ %s::geometry ORDER BY ST_Area(geom) LIMIT 1",
        (point_ewkt,),
    )
    smallest_enclosing = cur.fetchone()

functions.srid_notes: Planar box comparison in SRID units, with the usual mixed-SRID error. Note that box containment is not geometry containment: a concave polygon's box contains points that lie outside the polygon itself.

index_usage.notes: Directly index-assisted on the left operand. ST_Contains already generates this internally, so writing it yourself is only worthwhile in hand-tuned two-stage queries.

common_mistakes: Reading it as real containment. A crescent-shaped polygon's box contains the hole.
Confusing it with the PostgreSQL regex-match operator of the same spelling on text.
Getting the operand order backwards; ~ means 'A's box contains B's box', @ is the reverse.
